backgroundImageAltText: Medical education diagram with interactive hotspots

params.text:
The medial malleolus is the distal projection of the tibia, forming the inner bony prominence. Together with the lateral malleolus it creates the ankle mortise.

Clinical Pearl: Tenderness here triggers the Ottawa Ankle Rules for X-ray indication.

Memory Aid: "Mortise = Medial + lateral Malleoli + tibial roof."

The lateral malleolus extends more distally than the medial, which is why inversion injuries are more common.

Clinical Pearl: Weber classification of ankle fractures uses the fibular fracture level relative to the syndesmosis.

Memory Aid: "Lateral is Longer and Lower."

The deltoid ligament is a strong fan-shaped ligament with 4 parts resisting eversion.

Clinical Pearl: So strong that forced eversion more often causes an avulsion fracture rather than a tear. Isolated tear should raise suspicion for Maisonneuve fracture.

Memory Aid: "Deltoid = 4 bands fanning out like the Greek letter delta."

The ATFL is the weakest lateral ligament and the most commonly torn in ankle sprains.

Clinical Pearl: Anterior drawer test assesses ATFL integrity — pull the foot forward.

Memory Aid: "ATFL = Always Tears First Laterally."

The CFL is the second lateral ligament injured in progressive inversion sprains.

Clinical Pearl: Talar tilt test assesses CFL integrity. Tilt angle >10° more than uninjured side is positive.

Memory Aid: "CFL = Calcaneus-Fibula Link."
